original_hypothesis.title: Bid-Claim Timestamp Overlap Allows Orphaned Bids After Auction Finalization
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: participateToAuction uses block.timestamp <= endTime while claimAuction uses block.timestamp >= endTime. At equality, both execute. participateToAuction does NOT check auctionClaim. If claimAuction executes first (setting auctionClaim=true), a subsequent participateToAuction in the same block succeeds, creating an orphaned bid with no refund path: claimAuction can't be re-called, and cancelBid only works at timestamp <= endTime (possibly same block) but not after.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Missing auctionClaim check in participateToAuction combined with overlapping timestamp boundaries creates a window for irrecoverable bid deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: participateToAuction: block.timestamp <= endTime (line 58); claimAuction: block.timestamp >= endTime (line 105); no auctionClaim check in participateToAuction

preconditions: claimAuction executes before participateToAuction in same block
User's bid transaction also included in same block

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing auctionClaim check in bid function; overlapping timestamp boundaries
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Post-claim bid has no refund mechanism: claimAuction won't re-execute; cancelBid may fail after endTime block

analysis_questions: Can cancelBid work for the orphaned bid if called in the same block?
On L2, can multiple txs share the same block.timestamp?
Is there any recovery path for the orphaned bid?
validation_requirements: Execute claimAuction and participateToAuction in same block at endTime

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: At exact endTime, auction winner claims. In the same block, a user's bid transaction (submitted moments before) also executes. The bid succeeds (no auctionClaim check) but has no refund path since the auction is already claimed.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of the orphaned bidder's ETH
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possibly via cancelBid in same block only

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for concurrent bid and claim at boundary timestamp

validation_result.impact_summary: Bids placed in the same block as a claim at auction end time will be permanently locked in the contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `participateToAuction` function allows bidding as long as `block.timestamp <= minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)`. The `claimAuction` function allows claiming when `block.timestamp >= minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)`. At the exact block where `block.timestamp == minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)`, both conditions evaluate to true. If `claimAuction` is executed first in this block, it sets `auctionClaim[_tokenid] = true` and processes the auction settlement. If `participateToAuction` is executed subsequently in the same block, it succeeds because it does not check the `auctionClaim` status. The new bid is added to `auctionInfoData` and the user's ETH is deposited. However, because the auction is already claimed, `claimAuction` cannot be called again to refund this late bidder. Furthermore, once the block advances and `block.timestamp > minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)`, the `cancelBid` function will revert, leaving the user's ETH permanently locked in the contract.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows the overlapping timestamp conditions (`<=` and `>=`) and the missing `auctionClaim[_tokenid] == false` check in `participateToAuction`. The `cancelBid` function also strictly requires `block.timestamp <= minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)`, confirming that post-auction refunds are impossible.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function uses `<=` for the timestamp check and fails to verify if `auctionClaim[_tokenid] == false`, allowing bids to be placed even after the auction has been claimed in the same block.
The function uses `>=` for the timestamp check, creating an overlap with `participateToAuction` at the exact `endTime` block.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
function cancelBid(uint256 _tokenid, uint256 index) public {
        require(block.timestamp <= minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid), "Auction ended");
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Once the block timestamp exceeds `endTime`, users can no longer cancel their bids, meaning any orphaned bids from the `endTime` block are permanently locked.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: At `block.timestamp == endTime`, `claimAuction` is called, passing the `>=` check and setting `auctionClaim = true`. In the same block, `participateToAuction` is called, passing the `<=` check. It does not check `auctionClaim`, so it accepts the bid and ETH. In subsequent blocks, `cancelBid` reverts because `block.timestamp <= endTime` is false, and `claimAuction` reverts because `auctionClaim == false` is false.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is highly realistic as a race condition. Users often bid at the last possible moment to win an auction. The winner or an admin might also automate claiming the auction exactly at the end time. If both transactions land in the same block and the claim is ordered first, the late bidder's funds are permanently lost.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. Loss of user funds (ETH). Any bid placed in this specific window will be unrecoverable, leading to permanent lock of ETH in the contract.
recommended_mitigations: Change the timestamp check in `participateToAuction` to strictly less than (`<`), or change `claimAuction` to strictly greater than (`>`).
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the contracts and start an auction. 2. Fast forward the blockchain to exactly `auctionEndTime`. 3. Submit a `claimAuction` transaction followed by a `participateToAuction` transaction in the same block. 4. Observe that both succeed. 5. Fast forward the blockchain by one block. 6. Attempt to call `cancelBid` for the late bid and observe it reverts, confirming the funds are locked.
